paragraphs.body: I remember walking into a gym and seeing two courts next to one another. I was so anxious, having never played before, with the majority of girls having played. We all removed our outdoor shoes and put on court shoes to play. The coach yelled, "Circle up"! All 80 girls stood in a circle with the coaches at one end. The coaches already knew who I was but, having yet to meet them. Then they hey split us into groups based on skill. I only knew a few girls from my elementary school who happened to be in my group. Within those groups, we were given different drills based on skill. Having never played, I was in the low-skill group, so I had to partner to do simple drills like toss and catch. That simple drill got me hooked, and I have never looked back.

annotations.comment: Nice job starting with narrative! I wonder, though, can you use more concrete and specific--even sensory--details to bring this scene to life?
annotations.context_right: having never played before, with the majority of girls having played. We all removed our

paragraphs.body: Volleyball started as an activity my mom used to get me out of the house. I was hesitant, never enjoying any sports I played before. I went in with the assumption I was not going to like it, but to my surprise, I came up with the opposite opinion. Volleyball soon became a place I could look forward to. Those two hours of practice, twice a week, became a highlight of my week.

There was nothing complex about volleyball. There was no pressure, which created a safe environment where I could truly grow. When I first started my coaches cared not about winning but strictly about player growth, wanting to see players become their best. There was no punishment for making mistakes because coaches wanted you to learn. Everyone played in the games; there were no starters, because playing times was how you got better. This view of my sport changed quickly. Just over a year after I started playing, I decided to take an offer on a more competitive team. I soon learned this team only cared about winning. It was a toxic environment, putting girls against one another for a starting spot. Coaches would take you out of matches for one mistake if you were not a starter but keep their starters in no matter what. I was not a starter, but I quickly learned what that entailed. On this team, it meant coaches would lie to you, saying, "I am going to play you more next tournament" or "You have improved. I will play you more next time." When next time, it would be the same situation playing one set of the three matches you play just one day of the tournament. This was the start of hurtful coaches in my seven years of playing.

annotations.text: There was no pressure, which created a safe environment where I could truly grow. When I first started my coaches cared not about winning but strictly about player growth, wanting to see players become their best.
annotations.comment: you seem to be suggesting that these are good coaching philosophies? Yes? Let's talk :)

annotations.text: This was the start of hurtful coaches in my seven years of playing.
annotations.comment: Yes. You're definitely comparing and contrasting your two experiences, here. I wonder who your audience is for this essay? What is your essay's purpose? Let's talk :)

paragraphs.body: My high school years were some of the worst years I faced while playing. I joined the program in eighth grade, two years after I started playing. I did not know much before joining; I had just moved and was not in high school yet. I made the team and played on JV for my eighth and freshman years. I played all summer before sophomore year, hoping to make varsity. Tryouts rolled around, and there were four spots open; he took three girls and left me on JV. When he told me, he said, "We don't have room for you on the team," and "You will practice with varsity sometimes because we need you for next year." I later found out he took a girl who did not go to tryouts because she was sick. My coach already knew who he wanted but it led me to believe I stood a chance. I practiced with varsity once in a while that year, each time being extremely anxious because I was not on the team. "What if the girls didn't want me there?" or if "I wasn't good enough" to be playing is what started to play in my head.

annotations.text: My coach already knew who he wanted but it led me to believe
annotations.comment: You're repeating the idea of coaches "lying" or not telling the whole truth.

paragraphs.body: I was on the team starting the following year, but I later realized what that came with. We were not as good as we had been in the past that year, having a tied record by the end of the season. My coach blamed a singular player for the loss, sometimes even if we won. He would say, "You need to be doing better," or "What is going on?"

By my senior year, I was used to what he had to say to players. I thought I knew everything that could happen during this season, but I was completely wrong. That year, a junior transfer played my position and ended up beating me out for it. My coach split the team in half, with teammates taking sides for me or the other girl. When it was announced, he did it in front of the whole team, just saying the starting lineup, "Sam as defensive specialist and Olivia as libero." He never said anything to me until I had to approach him because I was not playing well with her. After all, we were both controlling on the court due to our positions. When I briefly mentioned at the end of our conversation how he announced the positions hurt me, he said he "didn't even realize." I had played under him for five years at the time, and he made me feel like a terrible player and not respected as a person.

When I think of volleyball now, I think of how it grew me as a person. Those bad coaches who made me start to hate the sport I once loved made me a stronger person. Never having been a starter until later in my career I am a hard worker. Being told I was not good enough I gained resilience. Behind all the bad there were some good coaches who gave me confidence as a player and a person.

annotations.text: Those bad coaches who made me start to hate the sport I once loved made me a stronger person. Never having been a starter until later in my career I am a hard worker. Being told I was not good enough I gained resilience. Behind all the bad there were some good coaches who gave me confidence as a player and a person.
annotations.comment: I'm interested! Might these ideas be at the heart of this essay? Perhaps something about the value of sports? Let's discuss.

paragraphs.body: I did those toss and pass drills until my last practice ever. They evolved a little over the years passing on my side, having to shuffle to the ball and even passing back and forth occasionally. Those drills at heart were simple. The were easy at the end you didnt have to think about what you were doing, it was just volleyball.

annotations.text: Those drills at heart were simple. The were easy at the end you didnt have to think about what you were doing, it was just volleyball.
